title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: Four blockers must be fixed before this merges. The charge handler dumps the entire request body — raw card number, CVC, and all — via console.log, a straight PCI-DSS violation. A bare catch-TODO silences every coupon failure so bad input looks like success. The discount_amount in the response is always zero: payload.amount is mutated to finalAmount before the subtraction, so the diff is permanently 0. And the couponCode is read from the raw body before Zod parses it, bypassing schema validation entirely. Beyond the blockers: six static coupons have been wrapped in three classes, an abstract base, and a factory that add zero capability; doStuff, theData, tmp, result2 are not production names; response: any gags the compiler on the file's most critical output; and the PR explicitly defers its own tests.

findings.title: Full charge body (card PAN + CVC) logged to stdout
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG charge body:', JSON.stringify(theData))` serializes the entire request body — including card.number and card.cvc — to stdout before any redaction. PCI-DSS 3.x forbids storing or logging CVC anywhere, ever, and requires PAN to be masked. The project ships a pino instance with a redaction allowlist precisely to prevent this. Delete this line; if you need request tracing use the injected logger and log only safe fields such as `{ customerId, couponCode }`.

findings.title: couponCode read from raw body before Zod validation
findings.message: `const couponCode = theData.couponCode` extracts the value from the unvalidated `req.body` before `chargeSchema.parse()` runs. If chargeSchema does not include couponCode, it is completely unvalidated user input that reaches business logic. Even if it does, the raw string — not the schema-coerced/sanitized one — is what gets used and passed to `doStuff`. Always read derived values from `payload` (the parsed result), never from the raw body.

findings.title: Swallowed exception hides coupon failures silently
findings.message: `catch (e) { // TODO }` drops every error from `doStuff` on the floor. A malformed coupon, an unexpected throw from the strategy, a null dereference — all of them silently fall through and the charge proceeds as if nothing happened. The caller gets a 201 with no indication that coupon application failed. Either handle the error meaningfully (log + return a 422), or let it propagate to the outer catch. A TODO in a catch block is not error handling.

findings.title: discount_amount is always zero — mutation destroys original amount
findings.message: Line 24 writes `payload.amount = finalAmount`, which overwrites the original amount with the discounted one. Line 36 then computes `payload.amount - finalAmount`, which is `finalAmount - finalAmount = 0`. Every successful coupon response will report `discount_amount: 0`. Fix: capture the original before mutation — `const originalAmount = payload.amount` — and use `originalAmount - finalAmount` in the response.

findings.title: `const response: any` disables TypeScript on the charge response
findings.message: Casting `transaction` to `any` to bolt on extra fields is an escape hatch that turns off type checking for the most security-sensitive object in the file. Define an interface or use spread: `const response = { ...transaction, discountAmount, couponCode }` with a proper return type. The compiler was trying to tell you something about the shape; silence it with a type fix, not `as any`.

findings.title: Response fields use snake_case in a camelCase API
findings.message: `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` are snake_case. If the rest of the API surface is camelCase (as implied by fields like `customerId`, `couponCode` in the request), these response keys break the contract for every client. They will also silently never match a camelCase sort key if anyone downstream tries to order by them. Use `discountAmount` and `couponCode` to match house style.

findings.title: `console.log` bypasses structured logger and its redaction allowlist
findings.message: `console.log` skips pino's log levels, structured fields, and — critically — its redaction allowlist. Debug lines that escape review via `console.log` are a recurring data-leak vector. Use the injected `logger` for every emission; there should be zero bare `console.*` calls in a finished diff.

findings.title: Three-class OOP hierarchy for a six-row static lookup table
Abstract class with a single abstract method — this is just an interface
findings.message: `AbstractCouponStrategy` declares one `abstract apply()` and nothing else — no shared state, no concrete behavior. An abstract class earns its keep with concrete methods or shared fields. With none of those, it forces single inheritance for no benefit and locks out any class that already extends something. Replace with `interface CouponStrategy { apply(amount: number): number; }`.

findings.title: Exported entry point and local variables have meaningless names
findings.message: `doStuff` is the function name a caller sees from the import. It communicates nothing. `theData`, `tmp`, `result2`, `p`, `x`, `amt` are all one step above `a` and `b`. Rename: `doStuff` → `applyCoupon`; `tmp` → `strategy`; `result2` → `discountedAmount`; `p` → `percentOff`; `amt` → `flatAmountCents`. Names are the cheapest documentation and the first thing read.

findings.title: `var` and `==` throughout — inconsistent with TypeScript codebase conventions
findings.message: `var x = ...` (line 16), `var tmp = ...` (line 58), and `code == 'WELCOME10'` / `tmp == null` (lines 34, 59) use JS-legacy syntax that the linter (`prefer-const`, `eqeqeq`) should already reject. `var` has function scope, not block scope; `==` does type coercion. Replace all with `const`/`let` and `===`.

findings.title: Unknown coupon code echoed to stdout via console.log
findings.message: `console.log('DEBUG: unknown coupon code:', couponCode)` prints attacker-supplied input directly to stdout, bypassing the pino redaction allowlist. An adversary can use this to pollute structured log pipelines or probe valid coupon codes via timing/output differences. Use the injected logger at `warn` level and log only a safe representation: `logger.warn({ couponCode }, 'unknown coupon code')`.

findings.title: FixedCouponStrategy.apply() can return a negative amount
findings.message: `return amount - this.amt` produces a negative value when the coupon value exceeds the charge amount (e.g., TENOFF = 1000 cents applied to a $4 charge). A negative amount sent to the payment gateway will either error unpredictably or, worse, succeed with reversed polarity. Add a floor: `return Math.max(0, amount - this.amt)`.

findings.title: Commented-out dead code (FREESHIP)
findings.message: The `// else if (code == 'FREESHIP') ...` block is embalmed history. Nobody knows if it's a plan, a rollback, or a fossil, and every future reader has to stop and decide. Git remembers it — delete it.

findings.title: Zero tests for new coupon math — PR explicitly defers them
findings.message: The PR description marks "Add unit tests for the coupon math" as unchecked and calls it a TODO follow-up. New branching code on a money path ships with no coverage. This is not a small omission: the discount calculation has a correctness bug (discount always 0), a floor bug (negative amounts), and a silent error swallow — none of which any test would have caught. Unit tests for `applyCoupon` with valid codes, unknown codes, over-limit flat discounts, and zero-amount edge cases are required before this merges, not after.
